verses.text: Then Samuel took a vial of oil, and poured it upon his head, and kissed him, and said, Is it not because the LORD hath anointed thee to be captain over his inheritance? Guidance: A small flask, a private moment, a kiss of recognition. Samuel does not crown Saul; he anoints him. The vial of oil — perhaps the size of a small spice bottle — carries the appointment of an entire kingdom. The kiss is a covenant gesture, the same kind a Hebrew patriarch gave to confer succession. Samuel calls Saul 'captain over his inheritance' — not 'king,' which is the people's word. God will call him a captain (nagid), the people will demand a king (melek). The chapter opens with God's preferred title; the people will impose theirs later. Sometimes the office one accepts and the office God appoints are two slightly different jobs in the same hands.

When thou art departed from me today, then thou shalt find two men by Rachel's sepulchre in the border of Benjamin at Zelzah; and they will say unto thee, The asses which thou wentest to seek are found: and, lo, thy father hath left the care of the asses, and sorroweth for you, saying, What shall I do for my son? Then shalt thou go on forward from thence, and thou shalt come to the plain of Tabor, and there shall meet thee three men going up to God to Beth-el, one carrying three kids, and another carrying three loaves of bread, and another carrying a bottle of wine: and they will salute thee, and give thee two loaves of bread; which thou shalt receive of their hands. After that thou shalt come to the hill of God, where is the garrison of the Philistines: and it shall come to pass, when thou art come thither to the city, that thou shalt meet a company of prophets coming down from the high place with a psaltery, and a tabret, and a pipe, and a harp, before them; and they shall prophesy: and the Spirit of the LORD will come upon thee, and thou shalt prophesy with them, and shalt be turned into another man. Guidance: Three signs — three predictive proofs that this anointing is from the LORD, not just from Samuel. First sign: two men at Rachel's tomb will confirm the donkeys are found and the father's worry has shifted from livestock to son. Second sign: three men carrying ritual offerings to Bethel will hand Saul two of their three loaves — strangers feeding a future king on the road. Third sign: Saul will join a prophetic band and be Spirit-empowered to prophesy. Each sign is more intimate than the last — from rumor, to gift, to inward transformation. The final phrase is the chapter's hope and tragedy: 'shalt be turned into another man.' The Spirit will indeed turn him into another man; Saul will not stay turned.

When these signs are come unto thee, that thou do as occasion serve thee; for God is with thee. Thou shalt go down before me to Gilgal; and, behold, I will come down unto thee, to offer burnt offerings, and to sacrifice sacrifices of peace offerings: seven days shalt thou tarry, till I come to thee, and shew thee what thou shalt do. Guidance: Two charges. 'Do as occasion serve thee, for God is with thee' — a remarkable freedom; Saul is told to act decisively as opportunities arise, on the basis that God's presence guarantees the venture. The second charge is more pointed: seven days at Gilgal, wait for Samuel to make the offerings. This second instruction will become the chapter-13 trap: Saul will not wait, will offer himself, and lose the kingdom over it. The verse plants a test that will be triggered later. Faith both moves boldly and waits patiently; Saul will fail the second discipline.

It was so, that when he had turned his back to go from Samuel, God gave him another heart: and all those signs came to pass that day. When they came thither to the hill, behold, a company of prophets met him; and the Spirit of God came upon him, and he prophesied among them. It came to pass, when all that knew him beforetime saw that, behold, he prophesied among the prophets, then the people said one to another, What is this that is come unto the son of Kish? Is Saul also among the prophets? And one of the same place answered and said, But who is their father? Therefore it became a proverb, Is Saul also among the prophets? Guidance: 'God gave him another heart.' All three signs land that day, including the Spirit-empowered prophesying. The neighbors are baffled — the village donkey-loser is now speaking ecstatically with the country's prophets. 'Is Saul also among the prophets?' becomes a proverb of unexpected transformation. The line carries a second meaning, ironic in retrospect: by chapter 19, Saul will prophesy again, naked and prone, on his way to murder David. The proverb that begins with hope ends with mockery. The verse before us, though, is pure grace: God has given him a new heart. The tragedy will be that Saul does not keep the heart God gave him.

Saul's uncle said unto him and to his servant, Whither went ye? And he said, To seek the asses: and when we saw that they were no where, we came to Samuel. And Saul's uncle said, Tell me, I pray thee, what Samuel said unto you. And Saul said unto his uncle, He told us plainly that the asses were found. But of the matter of the kingdom, whereof Samuel spake, he told him not. Guidance: Saul's uncle (probably Ner, father of Abner) presses him for details. Saul tells the truth about the donkeys but holds back the kingdom news. The verse is read two ways: as humble restraint (he is not ready to trumpet his anointing) or as cowardly evasion (he is afraid to announce something so public). The narrator does not interpret, but Saul's repeated pattern of avoiding the throne (he hides in baggage in v22) suggests genuine reluctance. Sometimes calls go quiet between the private anointing and the public coronation; the gap can be either humility being formed or fear ripening.

Samuel called the people together unto the LORD to Mizpeh; and said unto the children of Israel, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, I brought up Israel out of Egypt, and delivered you out of the hand of the Egyptians, and out of the hand of all kingdoms, and of them that oppressed you: and ye have this day rejected your God, who himself saved you out of all your adversities and your tribulations; and ye have said unto him, Nay, but set a king over us. Now therefore present yourselves before the LORD by your tribes, and by your thousands. Guidance: Samuel gathers the people back to Mizpeh — the place of the repentance revival of chapter 7. The opening sermon is sharp: 'I brought you up… you have rejected your God.' Even in the moment of granting their request, God names what their request actually was. He will give them the king they asked for, but He will not let them forget what choosing a king instead of Him meant. There is no soft language for the rejection. The kindest thing a faithful leader does is refuse to euphemize what people have actually chosen — even while serving them what they chose.

When Samuel had caused all the tribes of Israel to come near, the tribe of Benjamin was taken. When he had caused the tribe of Benjamin to come near by their families, the family of Matri was taken, and Saul the son of Kish was taken: and when they sought him, he could not be found. Therefore they enquired of the LORD further, if the man should yet come thither. And the LORD answered, Behold, he hath hid himself among the stuff. They ran and fetched him thence: and when he stood among the people, he was higher than any of the people from his shoulders and upward. And Samuel said to all the people, See ye him whom the LORD hath chosen, that there is none like him among all the people? And all the people shouted, and said, God save the king. Guidance: The narrowing lot — tribe, family, man — confirms what Samuel already knew. The chapter's only comic moment: Saul has hidden himself in the baggage. The chosen king is literally cowering behind the suitcases. The LORD tells the assembly where he is; they pull him out. Samuel introduces him: 'See ye him whom the LORD hath chosen?' The people roar 'God save the king' — the chapter's first national acclamation. Saul's reluctance is on full display; the people's enthusiasm is in spite of it. He is the man Israel wanted; he is also the man God hand-picked. The book's tragedy is that both descriptions remain true simultaneously and the latter will not save him from the former.

Then Samuel told the people the manner of the kingdom, and wrote it in a book, and laid it up before the LORD. And Samuel sent all the people away, every man to his house. Guidance: Samuel does not just hand off the kingdom; he documents it. He writes 'the manner of the kingdom' — likely a constitutional charter codifying the warnings of chapter 8 and the king's covenant obligations from Deut 17. He lays the scroll 'before the LORD' — the kingdom is on file in heaven's records. A written constitution before the modern era. Samuel knew Saul would rule, but he also knew Saul could not rule without limits. Power without law is what every other nation had; the LORD's people would have a written law to which even the king answered. Then everyone goes home. The coronation does not produce a palace; it produces a paper trail.

Saul also went home to Gibeah; and there went with him a band of men, whose hearts God had touched. But the children of Belial said, How shall this man save us? And they despised him, and brought him no presents. But he held his peace. Guidance: Saul returns to his hometown, accompanied by 'a band of men whose hearts God had touched.' His first followers are gifts from God, not recruits from politics. But not everyone is on board: 'sons of Belial' (the recurring phrase for worthless men) sneer 'how shall this man save us?' and bring no tribute. Saul's response is striking: 'he held his peace.' He does not retaliate. The early Saul has a quietness about him that the late Saul will lose entirely. The chapter ends with the king-in-waiting choosing silence over revenge. The same option will be available to him many times later. It will not always be the choice he takes.

Saul also went home to Gibeah; and there went with him a band of men, whose hearts God had touched. But the children of Belial said, How shall this man save us? And they despised him, and brought him no presents. But he held his peace. Guidance: Saul returns to his hometown, accompanied by 'a band of men whose hearts God had touched.' His first followers are gifts from God, not recruits from politics. But not everyone is on board: 'sons of Belial' (the recurring phrase for worthless men) sneer 'how shall this man save us?' and bring no tribute. Saul's response is striking: 'he held his peace.' He does not retaliate. The early Saul has a quietness about him that the late Saul will lose entirely. The chapter ends with the king-in-waiting choosing silence over revenge. The same option will be available to him many times later. It will not always be the choice he takes.
